summary: Adversarially verify the voice/STT session-lifetime hardening diff — three independent skeptics per file

findings.title: WhisperKit: the stale-session belt tears down the SUCCESSOR's live capture — successor stream hangs forever
findings.interleaving: AudioProcessor has ONE shared engine slot, and stopRecording() (AudioProcessor.swift:1085-1104) tears down whatever engine occupies it. Interleaving (no exotic ordering needed): (1) Session N is live and transcribing speech (decode in-flight, onProgressCallback Tasks streaming). (2) stopListening(): claims under lock, chains pendingStop_N; the chained stopStreamTranscription() enters the actor at a suspension point of the in-flight transcribeAudioSamples (actors are reentrant) — sets isRecording=false, stopRecording() nils the slot, RETURNS; the chain is now RESOLVED, but the in-flight decode keeps running and its onProgressCallback Tasks (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:196-199) land later. (3) startListening() N+1: start task awaits the (resolved) chain, gen check passes, startStreamTranscription → startRecordingLive puts NEW engine B in the shared slot. Mic live. (4) A late onProgressCallback Task from streamer N fires with non-empty progress.text → state.currentText didSet → provider onState with generation N → text non-empty so it survives the guard → gen mismatch → THE BELT: Task { streamerN.stopStreamTranscription() } → audioProcessor.stopRecording() → guard let engine = slot → ENGINE B (session N+1's!) → removeTap/disconnect/stop/reset, slot=nil. (5) Session N+1's realtimeLoop keeps running (its own isRecording is still true) but audioSamples never grows → transcribeCurrentBuffer sleeps 100ms forever ('Waiting for speech...'); no error is thrown, so the catch/stopListening path in WhisperKitProvider.swift:231-239 never fires. The consumer awaits an AsyncStream that never yields and never finishes; mic torn down with no owner to restart it. The file's safety comment at WhisperKitProvider.swift:183-186 ('stopRecording nils the engine ... safe') is only true while the slot is nil — false once a successor re-arms it.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the belt structurally unable to touch a slot it doesn't own. Preferred shape: close the ordering hole instead (see finding 2) with a per-session 'started-or-abandoned' gate that the chained stop awaits, then DELETE the belt — its only justification is the ordering the chain can't promise. If the belt must stay: fire it at most once per StreamerBox, and only after re-checking under `lock` that no live session exists (e.g., self.streamer == nil AND generation hasn't been re-claimed by a start); never call stopStreamTranscription from a stale callback while a successor session is live.

findings.title: WhisperKit: chained stop can be LOST inside startStreamTranscription's permission suspension — hot mic with no owner, belt can't fire in silence
findings.interleaving: startStreamTranscription (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:73-87) suspends at `await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission()` (an async call even when already authorized — AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission) BEFORE setting isRecording=true. Interleaving: (1) Session N start task passes the gen check (WhisperKitProvider.swift:229) and enters startStreamTranscription; suspends at the permission await. (2) stopListening(nil) claims: gen bump, continuation finished, chained pendingStop_N runs — actor reentrancy lets stopStreamTranscription execute DURING the permission suspension: isRecording=false (already false — the stop is a no-op) and stopRecording() (slot nil — no-op). The chain RESOLVES. (3) The permission await resumes: isRecording=true, startRecordingLive arms a new engine, realtimeLoop runs forever. Result: mic recording with NO owner — generation superseded, self.streamer nil (so no future stop chain ever reaches this streamer), pendingStop resolved, continuation already finished. The belt cannot save it in silence: the provider's onState guard `!text.isEmpty, text != "Waiting for speech..."` (WhisperKitProvider.swift:173) returns BEFORE the generation check, and in silence currentText only ever holds ""/the placeholder — so the hot mic persists indefinitely until someone speaks (at which point the belt fires and, per finding 1, may kill a live successor's engine instead of just the zombie). Privacy-critical: mic indicator on, app believes listening is stopped.
findings.suggested_fix: Order stop-after-start structurally: give each session a write-once 'started-or-abandoned' async gate (e.g., a CheckedContinuation resumed either (a) in onState BEFORE the text guard the first time newState.isRecording is true, or (b) by the start task when it returns without starting — gen-check fail or throw). The chained teardown becomes: await previous; await startGate; await stopped?.stopStreamTranscription(). With stop guaranteed to land after start on the actor, the lost-stop window closes and the belt (finding 1) can be deleted.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: stale begin vs successor begin can double-install the mic tap — AVFAudio NSException crash (or, in the sibling ordering, the stale unwind strips the LIVE session's tap)
findings.interleaving: begin()'s stillCurrent check (AppleSpeechTranscriber.swift:175-179) and its tap install (line 192) are separated by an engineLock acquisition, and the install is unconditional. Quick restart (startListening N+1 while begin_N is between its stillCurrent check and the install): (1) begin_N passes stillCurrent (gen still N), then blocks on engineLock — naturally held by startListening N+1's stopListening engine pass (engine.stop() drains tap callbacks, ms-scale). (2) The stop's pass removes taps (none yet) and releases. (3) begin_N acquires engineLock, installs tap A — it is already stale but hasn't re-checked. (4) begin_N proceeds into slow XPC (recognizer.recognitionTask creation, line 200) before reaching its taskAccepted unwind. (5) begin_{N+1} (auth cached → synchronous true, stillCurrent passes at gen N+1) reaches ITS engineLock install with tap A still on bus 0 → installTap over an existing tap → AVFAudio 'required condition is false: nullptr == Tap()' NSException → process crash. Sibling ordering [install A → stop-pass removes A → install B → begin_N's taskAccepted-false unwind (line 234) removeTap]: the stale unwind silently removes the SUCCESSOR's tap B — engine runs with no tap, the recogniser gets no audio, the successor's stream yields nothing and finishes only if Speech ever times out. The unwind comment ('the tap we installed is gone or going; removing again is the same idempotent drain', lines 229-231) is false once a successor has installed a new tap.
findings.suggested_fix: Stamp engine ownership: record the owning generation under `lock` when a session arms, and make every engineLock critical section that begin_N's stale paths run (the taskAccepted-false unwind, and the install itself) re-verify ownership before touching the engine — e.g., re-check `generation == self.generation` under `lock` immediately before installTap inside the engineLock hold, and route stale-begin cleanup through the claiming stop path (which already owns removal) instead of raw unconditional removeTap. Alternatively serialize begin's arm sequence and stop's engine pass on one serial executor so a stale begin's engine ops can never interleave with a successor's.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: the commit-point epilogue can stop the SUCCESSOR's running engine and strip its tap
findings.interleaving: The epilogue (AppleSpeechTranscriber.swift:256-265) assumes 'we are the only ones holding the pieces' — false once a successor re-arms the shared engine. Interleaving: (1) begin_N passes taskAccepted (gen N), engineLock { prepare; start } → engine RUNNING; thread descheduled between releasing engineLock and acquiring `lock` for the staleAfterStart read. (2) startListening N+1: stopListening claims (gen bump), engine pass stops the engine + removes tap A. (3) begin_{N+1} fully arms: auth cached (sync), tap B installed, task stored, engine.start → running, its own epilogue reads current → session N+1 live. (4) begin_N resumes: staleAfterStart reads gen mismatch → true → engineLock { audioEngine.stop() — stops the LIVE successor's engine; removeTap — removes tap B }. Session N+1 is silently dead: engine stopped, tap gone, no owner will restart it (the route-change observer only fires on hardware config changes, and handleConfigurationChange's engine restart requires a notification that a plain stop() does not reliably post). Its continuation stays open until SFSpeechRecognizer happens to error — potentially never on on-device recognition with no audio. Consumer awaits forever; the exact 'engine with no owner' class the epilogue was added to close, inverted.
findings.suggested_fix: Same ownership stamp as finding 3: before the epilogue's engine teardown, re-check under `lock` whether a successor session now exists (self.request != nil at a NEWER generation). If a successor owns the engine, the epilogue must skip the engine ops (the successor's begin re-armed/restarted it and its stop will own the teardown) and only run the idempotent request/task/continuation cleanup for its own pieces. Only when NO successor exists (a plain stop bumped the generation) may the epilogue stop the engine — that is the orphan case it was written for.

perLens.onFaith: (a) AVFoundation's installTap-over-an-existing-tap raising the well-known 'required condition is false: nullptr == Tap()' NSException — standard AVAudioEngine behavior, not runtime-tested here; if it were somehow tolerated, finding 3's crash ordering degrades to the same deaf-session harm as its sibling ordering. (b) removeTap(onBus:) on a bus with no tap being a safe no-op (the code relies on it in three places). (c) AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange never being posted synchronously on the thread calling engine.stop()/start() under engineLock — if it ever were, handleConfigurationChange's engineLock acquisition would self-deadlock on the non-reentrant NSLock; Apple's documented delivery is from an internal thread, but I could not prove it from source. (d) SFSpeechRecognizer.requestAuthorization / AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission callbacks always resuming their continuations exactly once. (e) An orphaned AVAudioEngine being stopped promptly by ARC dealloc when startRecordingLive replaces the slot. None of the four findings depend on (b)-(e).

findings.title: WhisperKit: zombie streamer records the mic forever with no owner — the belt is dead during silence because the text filter runs BEFORE the generation check
findings.interleaving: 1) startListening → session A (gen N); A's start Task awaits previousStop (done), passes `lock.withLock({ generation == self.generation })`. 2) stopListening() claims A: bumps gen to N+1, chains pendingStop = Task{ await prev; await A.streamer.stopStreamTranscription() }. 3) The chained stop reaches the actor FIRST (or interleaves while A's startStreamTranscription is suspended at `await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission()` — the actor is free there): it sets isRecording=false (already false) and calls audioProcessor.stopRecording() which no-ops (`guard let engine = audioEngine else { return }`, engine is nil). The chain Task COMPLETES. 4) A's startStreamTranscription resumes: isRecording guard passes (false), sets isRecording=true, startRecordingLive → mic RECORDING, realtimeLoop spins. Nothing will ever stop it: the provider's state was already claimed, pendingStop already ran. 5) The designed mitigation — the belt in onState — never fires: in a silent room AudioStreamTranscriber sets currentText to "Waiting for speech..." (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:133-136,148-150), and WhisperKitProvider.swift:173 `guard !text.isEmpty, text != "Waiting for speech..." else { return }` returns BEFORE the generation check at line 174 — so the stale-session branch (line 179-188) is unreachable until the mic hears actual speech. Result: hot mic, isRecording loop spinning at 100ms/iteration, no owning session, indefinitely. The file's own comment (line 183-185) admits the chain "cannot promise" this ordering and names the belt as the mitigation — the belt does not cover the silence case.
findings.suggested_fix: In WhisperKitProvider.swift onState (lines 169-190): perform the generation check FIRST, before the empty/placeholder text filters — a stale session must stop its streamer on the very first state-change callback (bufferEnergy updates fire didSet constantly once recording, so the belt would trigger within ~100ms even in silence). Keep the text filters only on the live-session yield path. Longer term: give each session its OWN AudioProcessor instance (AudioStreamTranscriber accepts `audioProcessor:` — stop sharing kit.audioProcessor), which dissolves this entire shared-capture race class.

findings.title: WhisperKit: the belt's stopStreamTranscription tears down the SUCCESSOR session's live capture via the shared audioProcessor — 'stopRecording is a no-op' is false cross-session
findings.interleaving: Precondition: zombie session A recording (the interleaving above; A's chained stop already completed as a no-op). 1) New session C starts: stopListening() claims (streamer nil, chain no-op), C's start Task awaits the completed chain, passes its gen guard, startStreamTranscription → startRecordingLive replaces audioProcessor.audioEngine with C's fresh engine (AudioProcessor.swift:1036). C is live and capturing. 2) Zombie A's realtimeLoop is STILL spinning (A's actor isRecording=true) reading the SAME shared audioProcessor.audioSamples — now C's audio. 3) User speaks. A transcribes the shared buffer; A's onState fires with non-empty text; gen check fails (A stale) → THE BELT fires: `Task { await streamerBox.value?.stopStreamTranscription() }` (WhisperKitProvider.swift:186) → sets A.isRecording=false (good, kills A's loop) AND calls audioProcessor.stopRecording() → AudioProcessor.swift:1085-1104: `guard let engine = audioEngine` — that is C's ENGINE — removeTap, disconnectNodeInput, engine.stop(), engine.reset(), audioEngine=nil. 4) C's capture is dead mid-utterance. C's realtimeLoop keeps spinning on a frozen sample buffer emitting "Waiting for speech..." forever; C's continuation never yields again; the UI believes it is listening. The in-code justification (lines 184-186: "a second stop on an already-stopped streamer is a no-op (stopRecording nils the engine)") is only true within one streamer — stopRecording is scoped to the shared processor, not the calling streamer, verified in AudioProcessor source.
findings.suggested_fix: The belt must not touch shared capture it no longer owns. Route the belt's stop through provider state under `lock`: only call stopStreamTranscription directly if no live session has since started capture (e.g., track a 'capture owner' generation set when a start Task actually reaches startStreamTranscription; the belt checks it and, if a successor owns capture, only needs to kill its own loop). Since AudioStreamTranscriber offers no loop-only stop, the clean structural fix is per-session AudioProcessor instances (constructor already takes one), making stopRecording inherently session-scoped — that fixes findings 1 and 2 together.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: stale begin()'s unwind removeTap and commit-point epilogue operate on the shared engine without ownership check — can strip the successor's tap (deaf session), stop the successor's running engine, or crash on double installTap
findings.interleaving: Widest window (unwind variant): 1) Session A begin(): passes stillCurrent (gen A), installs tap under engineLock, then enters `recognizer.recognitionTask(with:)` — real milliseconds of XPC to speechd. 2) stopListening() claims A (bumps gen), engine pass removes A's tap. 3) startListening → session B; begin(B) runs fast (authorized fast-path, request stored, TAP INSTALLED under engineLock). 4) A's recognitionTask creation returns; taskAccepted fails (gen moved); the unwind at AppleSpeechTranscriber.swift:234 does `engineLock.withLock { audioEngine.inputNode.removeTap(onBus: 0) }` — removing B's tap. Its comment (line 230-231: "the tap we installed is gone or going; removing again is the same idempotent drain") ignores that a successor may have re-installed. 5) B's begin proceeds: taskAccepted OK, engine.start() succeeds, epilogue sees gen current → no teardown. Result: engine RUNNING, no tap, session B permanently deaf (nothing re-installs absent a route-change notification); its recognition task never receives audio. Flipped order = crash: if A is preempted between its stillCurrent check (line 175) and its engineLock acquisition (line 192), and stop+B-start+B-tap-install complete in that gap, A's installTap on the occupied bus raises the CoreAudio NSException (required condition is false: nullptr == Tap()) — there is no generation re-check inside the engineLock hold at install time. Epilogue variant (narrower): stop claims A between engine.start() returning and the staleAfterStart read (adjacent lock acquisitions); B fully re-arms (tap + engine.start); A's stale epilogue (lines 258-261) then executes audioEngine.stop() + removeTap — killing B's RUNNING engine. The epilogue's comment "We are the only ones holding the pieces" is false once a successor has re-armed the shared engine.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the shared-engine ops in the stale paths ownership-guarded. Establish the consistent order engineLock→lock (nothing currently holds lock while acquiring engineLock, and the tap callback takes only lock, so this is deadlock-safe) and inside each engineLock critical section of (a) begin's tap install, (b) the taskAccepted unwind, and (c) the commit-point epilogue, re-read `self.generation` under lock: install only if still current; skip removeTap/engine.stop entirely if a NEWER generation has stored a request (the successor now owns the engine and its own stop path will drain it — the racing stop already removed THIS session's tap). Alternatively track an explicit `engineOwnerGeneration` field updated under engineLock.

findings.title: WhisperKit: the start Task's catch cannot see mid-session stream failures — realtimeLoop swallows transcribe errors and returns normally, leaving the mic recording with a dead loop (owned, but the stated contract is wrong)
findings.interleaving: Session live and recording → a CoreML/decoder error inside transcribeCurrentBuffer → AudioStreamTranscriber.realtimeLoop catches it and `break`s (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:99-102) → startStreamTranscription RETURNS normally → the provider's `do { try await streamer.startStreamTranscription() } catch { ... stopListening(ifGeneration:) }` (WhisperKitProvider.swift:230-239) completes WITHOUT entering the catch. isRecording stays true, audioProcessor keeps capturing, the loop is gone: the session goes permanently dead-air while the mic stays hot, until an external stopListening (silence endpointer / user) cleans it up. Not an unowned leak — the session still owns the capture — but the comment's claim that mid-session failures ("CoreML hiccup, audio route change, decoder error") are logged and torn down here is unimplementable with this WhisperKit version: only startRecordingLive throws reach the catch.
findings.suggested_fix: Treat a normal RETURN of startStreamTranscription while the session is still current as end-of-stream: after the try await (outside the catch), call `stopListening(ifGeneration: generation)` unconditionally — it is generation-guarded so a superseded session no-ops. Update the comment to say the catch covers capture-setup failures only.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: an in-flight route-change notification from a claimed observer can arm a tap against a successor mid-begin (ultra-narrow double-installTap crash window)
findings.interleaving: removeObserver (stopListening line 136) does not cancel a delivery already executing. 1) Session A live; a route-change notification is in flight on some queue. 2) stopListening claims A (removes observer — too late for the in-flight one), startListening → session B; begin(B) stores request (line 175-179) but has not yet installed its tap. 3) The stale handleConfigurationChange runs: its only guard is `request != nil` (line 317) — true, it is B's request — no generation scoping. Under engineLock it removeTaps (none), sees engine not running, and installInputTap() INSTALLS a tap. 4) B's begin reaches its own installInputTap → second tap on bus 0 → CoreAudio NSException. Requires a route change racing a stop+restart within a millisecond-scale window, so very rare — but it is the one remaining engine-mutation path with no generation identity at all.
findings.suggested_fix: Capture the generation in handleConfigurationChange's registration (it already receives `ifGeneration` at observe time — thread it into the handler closure) and check `lock.withLock { generation == self.generation && request != nil }` before touching the engine.

perLens.onFaith: (1) AVAudioNode.removeTap on a bus with no tap is a safe no-op — relied on by four paths in AppleSpeechTranscriber; matches universally observed AVFoundation behavior but is not an explicit documented promise. (2) installTap on an already-tapped bus raises the CoreAudio NSException (basis of the crash variants) — widely observed, not re-proven here. (3) AVAudioEngine stops capture when deallocated (relevant when startRecordingLive replaces the zombie's engine reference in the finding-2 scenario; if it does NOT, that scenario is briefly worse — two engines capturing). (4) SFSpeechRecognizer callback delivery semantics (serial delivery; cancel produces an error callback; isFinal and error are the two terminal shapes) — assumed per Apple's contract. (5) AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission's fast-path behavior when already authorized (assumed it still costs at least an actor hop; the finding-1 interleaving does not depend on it — the chained-stop-wins-the-actor-first ordering suffices). (6) Did not build or run anything — these paths are verify-by-launch per the repo's own convention; this is a static adversarial pass as tasked.

findings.title: WhisperKit: zombie streamer records with no owner (hot mic after stop) — the belt is dead on silence because the text guard precedes the generation check
findings.interleaving: 1) startListening (gen N): streamer S1 accepted; inner Task T1 (WhisperKitProvider.swift:223) awaits previousStop (already complete), passes the gen check at :229 (N == N). 2) User calls stopListening(): claim bumps gen to N+1, captures S1, chains P1 = Task { await prev; await S1.stopStreamTranscription() } (:264-268), yields final + finishes the continuation — consumer looks cleanly stopped. 3) Race the design admits it cannot order: P1's stopStreamTranscription() lands on S1's actor BEFORE T1's startStreamTranscription() — it sets isRecording=false (already false) and calls audioProcessor.stopRecording() on a nil engine (no-op), then completes. Alternatively, T1's start wins the enqueue but suspends at `await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission()` (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:75, BEFORE isRecording=true at :79) and actor reentrancy lets P1's stop interleave there — same outcome. 4) T1's start proceeds: isRecording=true, startRecordingLive creates an engine — MIC ON, realtimeLoop spins. Nothing will ever stop it: the chain link already ran. 5) The belt (WhisperKitProvider.swift:186) is the only remaining defense, but onState returns at :173 (`guard !text.isEmpty, text != "Waiting for speech..."`) BEFORE the generation check at :174 — and with the user silent (they just pressed stop!), currentText stays ""/"Waiting for speech..." forever (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:133-136, 148-150; bufferEnergy state changes fire the callback constantly but always exit at the text guard). The mic stays hot indefinitely with no owner in a privacy-first product; even if the user later speaks near the mic, that post-stop audio is transcribed by the zombie before the belt finally fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Two parts. (a) Move the generation check in onState ahead of the empty/placeholder text guard so the belt fires on ANY stale state change (bufferEnergy/placeholder updates arrive continuously — the zombie is caught within ~100ms even in silence). (b) Close the ordering hole structurally: make the session's startStreamTranscription itself a link in the pendingStop chain (created under the same lock hold that accepts the streamer), so any subsequent stop chains strictly AFTER the start and stop-before-start becomes impossible; the start link should re-check generation as its first action. Note (a) alone is insufficient (see next finding: the belt's stop harms a live successor) and (b) alone is insufficient (actor-reentrancy at requestRecordPermission still needs the start link to hold the ordering, which chaining provides since links run to completion on the chain task, not interleaved on the streamer actor... verify the permission await: either pre-authorize before building the streamer or re-check isRecording after the await).

findings.title: WhisperKit: the belt's stopStreamTranscription() stops the SHARED AudioProcessor and kills a live successor session's capture — the 'double-stop is a no-op' comment is false when a newer session is recording
findings.interleaving: Continue from the zombie state (previous finding): S1 (gen N, stale) is recording via the shared kit.audioProcessor. 1) User starts a new session: stopListening claims (streamer nil, chain no-op), new gen M, streamer S2 accepted; T2 awaits the completed chain, gen check passes, S2.startStreamTranscription() → startRecordingLive replaces audioProcessor.audioEngine with S2's engine (AudioProcessor.swift:1036); S2 is the live session. Both S1's and S2's realtimeLoops now poll the SAME audioProcessor.audioSamples (every engine tap appends into the shared buffer — AudioProcessor.swift:908). 2) User speaks. S1's transcribeCurrentBuffer produces text → S1's onState fires → generation N != M → the belt runs `streamerBox.value?.stopStreamTranscription()` (WhisperKitProvider.swift:186). 3) stopStreamTranscription (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:89-93) sets S1.isRecording=false AND calls audioProcessor.stopRecording() — which tears down whatever engine is CURRENTLY stored, i.e. S2's live engine (removeTap, disconnect, stop, reset, audioEngine=nil — AudioProcessor.swift:1085-1104). 4) The live session S2 is now dead-air: its loop keeps polling a frozen buffer, currentText never grows, no error is thrown, the continuation never finishes — the user sees a listening UI that hears nothing until they manually stop. The provider comment at :183-185 justifies the belt with 'a second stop on an already-stopped streamer is a no-op (stopRecording nils the engine)' — that only holds when nothing newer is recording; stopRecording is processor-scoped, not streamer-scoped.
findings.suggested_fix: The belt must never call stopStreamTranscription inline on the shared processor. Instead, have the stale onState enqueue its streamer's teardown onto the pendingStop chain under lock (append `await streamerBox.value?.stopStreamTranscription()` as a chain link) so it serializes with session lifecycle — combined with the start-as-chain-link fix, a chained stale-stop can only run when no newer session's start is in front of it... but simplest robust form: track an owner generation for the audio processor under lock; the belt (and the chained stop) first re-checks under lock that no NEWER session currently owns the processor before calling stopRecording — if a newer session is live, only flip the stale streamer's isRecording (add a flag-only stop, or call stopStreamTranscription on a wrapper that skips audioProcessor.stopRecording when not owner).

findings.title: WhisperKit: a stale, delayed startStreamTranscription landing after a successor is live wipes the live session's audio buffer and orphans its engine (unstoppable capture)
findings.interleaving: 1) Session N: T1 passes its gen check at WhisperKitProvider.swift:229 but its actor hop to S1.startStreamTranscription() is delayed by scheduling (the check and the actor call are not atomic — anything can happen between :229 and :230). 2) stopListening claims (gen N→N+1); its chained P1 stop runs against the not-yet-started S1 (no-op, engine nil). 3) New session M starts fully: S2.startStreamTranscription() → S2's engine live, audioSamples accumulating mid-utterance. 4) T1's delayed call finally executes S1.startStreamTranscription(): S1.isRecording is false so the :74 guard passes; startRecordingLive runs `audioSamples = []` (AudioProcessor.swift:1031) — WIPING the live session's accumulated utterance mid-stream — and replaces audioProcessor.audioEngine with S1's new engine (:1036), orphaning S2's still-running engine whose tap keeps appending into the shared buffer alongside S1's tap (two taps, interleaved sample corruption for whatever S2 transcribes next). 5) Whichever engine is not stored in audioProcessor.audioEngine can no longer be stopped by ANY stopRecording call — when the eventual belt or a later session's chained stop fires, it stops only the stored engine; the orphaned one keeps the mic hot until the object happens to deallocate. Net: live-session transcript loss/corruption plus an unstoppable capture handle.
findings.suggested_fix: Same structural fix as finding 1(b): the session's start must be a link in the pendingStop chain created under the lock hold that accepted the streamer, so a claim's bump strictly precedes any later chain link and a stale start can never execute after a successor's start (the successor's start link runs after the stale session's stop link, which runs after the stale start link — total order). If chaining the start is unpalatable, at minimum re-check the generation INSIDE a lock read as the first statement executed on the streamer actor (pass a `isCurrent: () -> Bool` closure into a wrapper that guards startStreamTranscription), accepting that a race window remains between that check and startRecordingLive.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: handleConfigurationChange is generation-unscoped — a route-change handler that passed its liveness check before a stop's claim re-arms the engine AFTER the stop's teardown (ownerless hot mic), and a stale in-flight handler can double-install the tap into a starting session (CoreAudio NSException crash)
findings.interleaving: HOT-MIC VARIANT (wide window — the handler only needs to arrive within the milliseconds around a stop, and Bluetooth profile flips coincide with capture stop by nature): 1) Session live; a route change posts .AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange; the handler block (AppleSpeechTranscriber.swift:316) runs on the audio system's posting thread and passes `lock.withLock({ request != nil })` at :317 — true. 2) stopListening claims (nils request, :113-124) and takes engineLock for its engine pass (:132-135); the handler blocks waiting at engineLock (:319). 3) Stop finishes: engine stopped, tap removed, observer removed (:136 — removal does not stop the already-executing block), continuation finished. Session fully dead. 4) Handler acquires engineLock: removeTap (no-op), isRunning false → skips stop, installInputTap() succeeds, prepare, engine.start() (:319-329) — the MIC IS BACK ON with request nil (tap drops every buffer at :288), no task, no continuation, no observer. Nothing owns it; it stays hot until the next startListening/stopListening happens to run an engine pass — indefinitely if the user just closed voice mode. CRASH VARIANT: same stale in-flight handler instead interleaves with a NEW session's begin(): new begin stores its request (:175-179) → handler's :317 check passes on the NEW request → handler wins engineLock first: removeTap (empty), installInputTap installs a tap, starts engine → new begin then runs `engineLock.withLock({ installInputTap() })` (:192) on a bus that already has a tap → AVAudioEngine raises the well-known 'required condition is false: nullptr == Tap()' NSException → process crash.
findings.suggested_fix: Generation-scope the handler like everything else: capture the session generation when registering the observer (observeConfigurationChanges already has it) and pass it into handleConfigurationChange; INSIDE the engineLock hold, re-check `lock.withLock { generation == self.generation && request != nil }` before touching the engine (nesting a lock read inside engineLock is inversion-safe here: no path holds `lock` while acquiring engineLock — all lock.withLock scopes close first — and the tap callback takes only `lock`). The same in-hold re-check pattern also fixes the crash variant, since a stale handler then no-ops before installInputTap.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: stale engine passes (begin's taskAccepted unwind, the commit-point epilogue, stopListening's post-claim pass) act on the SHARED engine without ownership scoping and can kill/deafen a successor session
findings.interleaving: The engine has no generation; only state does. Three sites, same class (all need OS preemption of straight-line code plus a concurrent stop+restart, so narrower than the handler finding but the PR's own epilogue treats exactly these windows as real): (a) UNWIND at :227-236 — begin(N) installs its tap, is preempted before the taskAccepted check; a stop claims (gen N+1) and runs its engine pass; a NEW session (gen N+2) begins: stores request, installs ITS tap under engineLock; old begin resumes, taskAccepted=false, and executes `engineLock.withLock { audioEngine.inputNode.removeTap(onBus: 0) }` at :234 — removing the SUCCESSOR's freshly-installed tap. New begin then starts the engine: mic indicator on, zero buffers delivered, recognizer waits forever, stream never finishes. The comment ('the tap we installed is gone or going; removing again is the same idempotent drain') never considers a newer session's tap in between. (b) EPILOGUE at :256-265 — begin(N) starts the engine, reads staleAfterStart=true (a stop landed), but before its engineLock teardown a full successor session (gen N+2) installs its tap and starts the engine; the epilogue then stops the successor's running engine and removes its tap — new session dead-air, no finish, no error. (c) stopListening's pass at :132-135 — a public stop's thread is preempted between its claim and its engine pass; a concurrent startListening + its begin arm and start the engine; the resumed stale pass stops it. In all three the successor is silently deaf/dead until the user manually stops.
findings.suggested_fix: Give the engine an owner: an `engineOwnerGeneration: UInt64` mutated only under engineLock. begin's install/start records its generation as owner; every teardown pass (stopListening :132, unwind :234, epilogue :258, and the route handler) re-checks INSIDE the engineLock hold that the owner is still the generation it is tearing down (for the nil-expected public stop: the generation it claimed) and no-ops if a newer generation has re-armed. The epilogue's self-cleanup remains correct when it is still the owner; when a successor owns the engine, the epilogue only needs endAudio/cancel/finish on its own pieces.

perLens.onFaith: (1) AVAudioEngine raises an NSException ('required condition is false: nullptr == Tap()') when installing a second tap on an occupied bus, and removeTap on an empty bus is a safe no-op — both are widely-documented AVFoundation behavior but not exercised here. (2) NotificationCenter.removeObserver does not interrupt an already-executing observer block, and queue:nil blocks run synchronously on the posting thread. (3) SFSpeechRecognizer semantics: cancel() surfaces as an error callback, requestAuthorization calls back exactly once, and a buffer-request recognition task starved of audio waits without erroring (basis for the 'deaf successor never finishes' claims). (4) AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission() is a genuine suspension point when already authorized (the actor-reentrancy variant of finding 1; the enqueue-race variant needs no such assumption). (5) An orphaned running AVAudioEngine's lifetime after its last strong ref drops (finding 3's 'unstoppable capture' tail; the buffer-wipe and engine-replacement halves are source-verified). (6) AsyncStream continuation finish/yield idempotence after termination, per stdlib documentation. I did not build or run anything — these paths are mic/TCC-gated per the repo's verify-by-launch convention, and this review is the stated gate.
